alsoKnownAs: Lesser Siren (Siren intermedia, sister species sold under the same trade name)

nativeRange.habitat: Slow or stagnant coastal-plain wetlands, vegetated ditches, swamps, and the benthic zone of seasonal/permanent ponds; thrives in waters with thick organic mats and dense aquatic vegetation.

decorPreferences: Heavily-planted or densely-decorated tank with multiple secure hides
Sunken PVC tubes, ceramic planters, or large pieces of mopani driftwood
Soft fine sand or smooth large gravel (avoid medium gravel — impaction risk during feeding)
Tightly-sealed weighted lid — sirens are powerful escape artists and will leave the tank at night through any gap

diet.notes: Predatory feeder that has been documented taking earthworms (a captive staple), small fish such as shiners and minnows, snails and freshwater clams (a wild favourite), crayfish, aquatic insects, and occasional pinky mice. Plant material and algae are often found in gut contents but appear incidental. Feed two or three times per week to satiation; uneaten food should be removed promptly to protect water quality.

avoidWith: Any fish small enough to be swallowed
Small ornamental shrimp and snails (treated as food)
Other sirens and amphiumas (cannibalism and inter-amphibian predation are documented)
Slow tankmates that cannot avoid an opportunistic predator

summary: Massive (up to ~95 cm) eel-like neotenic North American salamander. Fully aquatic, predatory, and long-lived; generally considered specialist-only because of adult size, escape behaviour, and a strict carnivorous diet.
careNotes: The Greater Siren is a fully aquatic, paedomorphic salamander that retains external gills for life and possesses only reduced front limbs (no hind limbs). Adults are commonly reported in the 50–80 cm range, with exceptional individuals approaching 95 cm and nearly a kilogram in weight, so reasonable adult housing starts around a 400 L (100 US gal) footprint and is generally advised to be larger for full-grown specimens. Water-quality stability matters more than precise chemistry — soft to moderately hard, neutral to slightly alkaline freshwater around 20–26 °C is well tolerated, and lighting can be dim. A tightly-sealed, weighted lid is non-negotiable: sirens have been reported to push through unsecured screen tops and have been known to survive surprisingly long out of water. Substrate should be either fine sand or smooth large gravel to avoid impaction during their suction-style strikes. They are reclusive, mostly nocturnal predators and have been reported to vocalise with clicks and yelps when handled. Wikipedia, Reptiles Magazine and Caudata Culture all confirm the species' identity and southeastern-US range; hobby-source water-parameter detail is sparse, so the pH/hardness ranges given here are typical for the coastal-plain wetlands the species inhabits rather than tightly-validated targets. Lesser Siren (Siren intermedia) is sometimes sold under the same trade names and has very similar requirements at a smaller adult size; this entry covers the species complex per project policy. Chytrid (Batrachochytrium dendrobatidis) has been documented in wild populations, so quarantine of any new acquisition is generally advised.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to breed in the wild from February to March, with males guarding clutches of roughly 500 eggs and fanning the nest until hatching at ~2 months. Captive breeding is essentially undocumented in the aquarium hobby; almost all specimens in the trade are wild-caught.

primary.name: Caudata Culture
primary.notes: Species identity, range (VA/NC/SC/GA/AL/FL), describer (Linnaeus 1766), IUCN status. Caudata Culture's full caresheet appears to be behind the broader species index; husbandry detail was cross-sourced from Reptiles Magazine and Wikipedia.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy, adult size (18–97 cm), 25-year captive lifespan, diet, behaviour, aestivation, and chytrid susceptibility.

additional.name: Reptiles Magazine — Greater Siren
additional.notes: Husbandry confirmation: 100 US gal (~380 L) recommended adult tank, ~75 °F (~24 °C), secure lid, gravel substrate, carnivorous diet of nightcrawlers/fish/crayfish, captive breeding effectively absent.

amphibian.breedingNotes: Wild breeding has been reported in late winter with paternal nest-guarding of ~500 eggs; aquarium breeding is essentially undocumented and is not a realistic goal for home keepers.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches up to ~95 cm as an adult, lives 20+ years, requires a 400 L+ tightly-sealed aquarium, is a powerful escape artist, and demands a strict live/whole-prey carnivorous diet. Inappropriate for typical home aquarists; suited to dedicated specialist keepers prepared for a multi-decade commitment.
